title: Architecture of Personality
subtitle: Intentional growth, skills, and future management
description: This checklist is the foundation of continuous development. It covers attention management, identity formation, working with limiting beliefs, and building a learning system. Without this, moving forward is chaotic.

items.title: Attention Management
items.text: The ability to concentrate on complex tasks without distraction is becoming the main superpower in the modern economy. Distracted attention reduces cognitive abilities and outcome quality.
items.tip: Practice 90-minute sessions of work without phone and notifications.

items.title: Inner Dialogue
items.text: How we talk to ourselves directly determines our stress level and problem-solving ability. Shifting from 'I' to 'you' or using your name reduces emotional involvement.
items.tip: In a stressful situation, ask yourself by name: '[Name], what specifically can be done right now?'

items.title: Identity vs. Goals
items.text: A goal is a temporary result. Identity is a stable system of beliefs about oneself. Changes stick long-term only when they become part of your personality.
items.tip: Formulate intentions not as 'I want to read a book' but as 'I am a reading person'.

items.title: Mental Models
items.text: Having a set of mental models from different disciplines (psychology, physics, biology) allows you to see reality in 3D and make non-obvious, correct decisions.
items.tip: Learn basic principles: inversion, compound interest, circle of competence, second-order consequences.

items.title: The Compound Effect of Improvements
items.text: Small daily actions (1% per day) seem unnoticeable in the moment, but over a year yield a 37-fold increase. The problem is that people overestimate one heroic leap and underestimate regularity.
items.tip: Choose one micro-skill (5 minutes reading, 10 pushups) and do it without skipping.

items.title: Working with Limiting Beliefs
items.text: A fixed mindset ('I don't have talent for languages') blocks development. A growth mindset ('I can't yet, but I can learn') opens up possibilities.
items.tip: Add the word 'yet' to any negative self-assessment: 'I can't do it... yet'.

items.title: Learning Strategy
items.text: Passive consumption of information creates an illusion of knowledge. Real learning requires active recall and application of knowledge in practice.
items.tip: After reading a chapter, close the book and write down the main idea in your own words on a piece of paper.

items.title: Reflection and Journaling
items.text: Unlived and unanalyzed experience is lost. Written evening reflection structures thinking and reduces anxiety levels.
items.tip: In the evening, write down 3 good events of the day and 1 action you can improve tomorrow.

items.title: Dopamine Management
items.text: Constantly seeking easy pleasure (social media, fast carbs) depletes dopamine receptors, robbing motivation for complex tasks. 'Dopamine fasting' practice restores sensitivity.
items.tip: Schedule one day a week without digital entertainment.

items.title: The Power of Environment
items.source: David Burkus — Friend of a Friend
items.text: We are the average of the five people we spend the most time with. Environment influences our behavior norms more than willpower.
items.tip: Consciously join a community of people who already have the skills you desire.

quiz.q: What is more effective for long-term change?
What is the key idea of 'Deep Work' by Cal Newport?
What yields a 37-fold increase over a year with 1% daily improvement?
How to combat the limiting belief 'I can't sell'?
What is an alternative to passive reading for memorization?
